title: Inkling: Reading the Model Card Across Checkpoints and Claims

central_claim.text: Inkling is a 66-layer sparse mixture-of-experts multimodal Transformer whose architecture, access options, checkpoints, and evaluation claims must be kept separate to understand what the release evidence actually supports.

tldr: Inkling is a 66-layer decoder-only sparse mixture-of-experts model with 975 billion total parameters and 41 billion active parameters per token path.
It accepts text, images, and audio, produces text, and advertises up to a one-million-token context window, while Tinker exposes smaller 64K and 256K options.
The release reports broad benchmark coverage and adjustable reasoning effort, but some results use internal harnesses or a checkpoint different from the released weights.

Open weights do not mean consumer-scale deployment: the mutable model card retrieved on 2026-07-18 listed 2 TB of aggregate VRAM for BF16 and 600 GB for NVFP4, while benchmark parity between those checkpoints is not established.

blocks.question: Why did Thinking Machines Lab release Inkling?
paragraphs: The release frames Inkling as a broad base model for customization rather than a model optimized to lead every benchmark. Its intended uses include agentic systems, coding assistants, chatbots, retrieval-augmented generation, instruction following, and multimodal applications.

That positioning matters because the provider explicitly says Inkling is not the strongest model overall. The proposed value is the combination of open weights, multimodal input, controllable reasoning effort, and availability for fine-tuning.

blocks.question: What is materially different about the release?
paragraphs: Inkling combines a large sparse model with native text, image, and audio input and makes the weights available in original and quantized forms. Only a subset of the 975 billion parameters is active for a token, which separates total model capacity from the 41-billion-parameter active path.

The release also exposes an effort control intended to trade generated tokens for performance. This is a product and training claim about controllability, not proof that Inkling dominates models at every effort level or task.

blocks.question: How does Inkling process a token and multiple input modalities?
paragraphs: Inkling is a decoder-only autoregressive Transformer with 66 layers. Its feed-forward backbone is a sparse mixture of experts: each token is routed to 6 of 256 routed experts, while 2 shared experts are always active. The selected and shared expert outputs are combined before the token continues through the network.

The release says local and global attention layers are interleaved at a 5-to-1 ratio with 8 key-value heads. Images and audio are converted into representations, projected into the same hidden space as text, and processed jointly by the decoder. The release describes 40-by-40 image patches through a four-layer hierarchical MLP and dMel spectrograms for audio; the website model card describes the same path more generally as a hierarchical patch encoder and discrete audio-token encoding.

The provider reports pretraining on 45 trillion tokens across text, images, audio, and video, followed by synthetic supervised fine-tuning and large-scale reinforcement learning. These are provider release claims; the model card discloses only broad data categories and provenance rather than a reproducible dataset inventory.

blocks.question: What would a local deployment decision look like?
paragraphs: Start by choosing a checkpoint, not by reading the phrase open weights as a hardware promise. In the content-addressed model-card retrieval from 2026-07-18, the BF16 checkpoint requires at least 2 TB of aggregate VRAM; the listed examples are 8 NVIDIA B300 GPUs or 16 H200 GPUs. The same snapshot gives a 600 GB minimum for NVFP4: W4A4 can use 4 B300 GPUs but requires SM100-or-newer architecture, while W4A16 can use 8 H200 GPUs. The live page remains mutable, so the retrieval hash in the source record must accompany these figures.

The quantized option reduces memory requirements, but the release does not identify the precision behind the main benchmark table or provide a BF16-versus-NVFP4 quality comparison. A deployment plan therefore cannot assume that the published benchmark values transfer unchanged to the quantized checkpoint.

blocks.question: What evidence does the release provide?
paragraphs: In the content-addressed provider-page retrievals from 2026-07-18, the main benchmark table reports Inkling at effort 0.99. Selected values include 29.7 percent on Humanity's Last Exam without tools, 46.0 percent with tools, 77.6 percent on SWE-bench Verified, 79.8 percent on IFBench, 73.5 percent on MMMU Pro Standard 10, 91.4 percent on VoiceBench, and 98.6 percent on StrongREJECT. These are vendor-published results across different benchmark families, not one common measurement scale; later live-page changes would require a new source hash.

The release states that the benchmark runs use temperature 1.0 and that coding evaluations allow trajectories up to 256K tokens. Some comparisons use externally reported scores; otherwise the provider uses internal harnesses. Inkling's Terminal Bench score uses an internal coding harness, contaminated web-search solutions were assigned zero, and VoiceBench required a formatting instruction because its grader is sensitive to output format.

The effort-sweep chart supports the narrower provider interpretation that performance can be traded against generated tokens. Its Humanity's Last Exam points came from an earlier checkpoint, and the release separately says its forecasting results came from testing on a checkpoint different from the released one.

blocks.question: Where should the claims be narrowed?
paragraphs: The provider says Inkling can hallucinate, fail to follow instructions, degrade in long multi-turn conversations, reproduce demographic or cultural biases, and perform unevenly across languages and subject domains. It recommends independent evaluation, application-level safeguards, and human oversight for high-stakes uses.

The one-million-token statement describes supported capacity, not demonstrated accuracy throughout that window. Tinker currently exposes 64K and 256K context options. The release also does not establish that the NVFP4 checkpoint matches BF16 benchmark quality or that base-checkpoint safety behavior survives downstream fine-tuning.

Training disclosure remains high level. The sources give broad categories and a 45-trillion-token release claim but not the dataset composition, rights breakdown, contamination analysis, language distribution, or training cutoff needed for a complete independent audit.

The model card, release page, and Acceptable Use Policy remain mutable live pages, but the exact official HTML retrieved on 2026-07-18 is content-addressed with SHA-256 and byte counts in the source records. Claims here describe those fixed retrievals; later page changes are not silently incorporated and require new hashes and review.

blocks.question: What should a careful reader conclude?
paragraphs: The release supports a strong descriptive conclusion: Inkling is a very large sparse multimodal model with downloadable checkpoints and broad vendor-reported evaluation coverage. It does not support calling the model best overall, consumer-deployable, fully open source, or proven at full quality across a one-million-token context.

Source boundaries are part of the evidence. The content-addressed website snapshot and initial checkpoint cards disagree on a few values, including GDPVal-AA and MMMU Pro. The exact model-card, release-page, and Acceptable Use Policy HTML retrieved on 2026-07-18 is identified by SHA-256 and byte count, while BF16 and NVFP4 are pinned to their initial release commits. The live pages remain mutable, but the source versions used here are reproducible.

The provider labels the model Apache 2.0, but its linked Model Acceptable Use Policy says that accessing or using the model materials is also conditioned on that policy. This explainer records both statements rather than treating open weights as a synonym for unrestricted use.

claims.text: In content-addressed official HTML retrieved on 2026-07-18, Thinking Machines Lab dates the initial Inkling release to July 15, 2026, labels the model Apache 2.0, and links an Acceptable Use Policy that says it also conditions use of the model materials.
Inkling is a 66-layer decoder-only sparse mixture-of-experts Transformer with 975 billion total parameters, 41 billion active parameters, 6 of 256 routed experts per token, and 2 shared experts.

The pinned initial BF16 card verifies text, image, and audio inputs with text output; the content-addressed provider pages retrieved on 2026-07-18 advertise support for a context window up to one million tokens.

The content-addressed model card retrieved on 2026-07-18 lists a minimum of 2 TB aggregate VRAM for BF16 and 600 GB for NVFP4, with distinct GPU and numeric-mode requirements; the checkpoint identities are pinned to their initial release commits.

In content-addressed pages retrieved on 2026-07-18, the provider positions Inkling as a broad customizable open-weights base with multimodal input and controllable effort, while explicitly stating that it is not the strongest model overall.

The content-addressed release page retrieved on 2026-07-18 reports pretraining on 45 trillion multimodal tokens and more than 30 million asynchronous reinforcement-learning rollouts after an initial synthetic supervised-fine-tuning stage.

In the content-addressed provider table retrieved on 2026-07-18, effort 0.99 results are 29.7 percent on Humanity's Last Exam without tools, 77.6 percent on SWE-bench Verified, 79.8 percent on IFBench, 73.5 percent on MMMU Pro Standard 10, 91.4 percent on VoiceBench, and 98.6 percent on StrongREJECT.

The content-addressed release page retrieved on 2026-07-18 interprets its effort sweep as evidence that Inkling can exchange generated tokens for performance and says it matches Nemotron 3 Ultra on Terminal Bench at roughly one-third of the generated tokens.

The content-addressed release page retrieved on 2026-07-18 says the forecasting results came from a checkpoint different from the released one and that the effort chart's Humanity's Last Exam points came from an earlier checkpoint.

The official checkpoint card warns about hallucinations, instruction-following failures, degraded long-conversation performance, uneven language or domain performance, and the need for independent evaluation and human oversight.

The provider's content-addressed website snapshot retrieved on 2026-07-18 and the immutable initial checkpoint cards disagree on some published values, including GDPVal-AA and MMMU Pro; the fixed source identities establish the disagreement but do not explain which values the provider intends as canonical.

The sources do not establish that NVFP4 preserves BF16 benchmark quality, that useful accuracy holds throughout a one-million-token prompt, or that base-model safety transfers unchanged to fine-tuned checkpoints.

visuals.title: Stored capacity and per-token activation are different quantities
visuals.question: What do 975 billion total and 41 billion active parameters mean?

items.label: 975B total parameters
items.detail: The stored sparse model contains the full collection of routed and shared expert parameters.

items.label: 41B active parameters
items.detail: For one token, the router selects 6 of 256 routed experts and also uses 2 shared experts, producing the reported active path.

items.label: Deployment implication
items.detail: Sparse activation reduces computation per token, but the downloadable checkpoint still has to represent the much larger stored model.

visuals.interpretation: The active parameter count describes the computation path for a token; it is not the checkpoint size or a promise that consumer hardware can hold the model.
limitations: Parameter counts alone do not determine throughput, latency, or memory under a specific serving implementation.
Checkpoint hardware requirements are reported separately in the deployment comparison.
visuals.alt_text: Parallel lanes distinguish Inkling's 975 billion stored parameters from its 41 billion active parameters per token. Six of 256 routed experts and two shared experts make up the active path, but the full checkpoint remains much larger.
visuals.fallback: Inkling stores 975 billion total parameters. A token activates a reported 41-billion-parameter path by using 6 of 256 routed experts plus 2 shared experts. Sparse activation does not make the stored checkpoint only 41 billion parameters.

visuals.title: Three input paths meet in one autoregressive decoder
visuals.question: How do text, images, and audio enter Inkling's shared hidden space?

items.label: Text tokens
items.detail: Text begins as token representations in the decoder's hidden space.

items.label: Image patches
items.detail: The release describes 40-by-40 patches passing through a four-layer hierarchical MLP before projection into the shared space.

items.label: Audio representation
items.detail: The release describes dMel spectrograms, while the website model card gives the broader description of discrete audio-token encoding.

items.label: Interleaved attention stack
items.detail: Local and global attention layers are reported at a 5-to-1 ratio with 8 key-value heads.

items.label: Text output
items.detail: The 66-layer decoder processes the shared representations autoregressively and emits text.

visuals.interpretation: Inkling uses modality-specific front ends, but their representations are projected into one decoder space rather than processed by three independent output systems.
limitations: The two provider pages describe the image and audio front ends at different levels of specificity.
The sources do not provide enough implementation detail to reconstruct exact tensor shapes or training data flow.
visuals.alt_text: Architecture layers show text tokens, image patches processed by a hierarchical MLP, and audio representations entering a shared hidden space, followed by interleaved local and global attention in a 66-layer autoregressive decoder that produces text.
visuals.fallback: Text, image, and audio each use an input representation path. Those representations enter one shared decoder space, where local and global attention layers are interleaved and the 66-layer autoregressive model produces text.

visuals.title: The benchmark snapshot spans non-equivalent tasks
visuals.question: What does the provider report at effort 0.99?

items.label: Humanity's Last Exam · no tools
items.detail: Vendor-reported percent; not directly comparable with the other benchmark families.

items.label: SWE-bench Verified
items.detail: Vendor-reported coding benchmark percent.

items.label: IFBench
items.detail: Vendor-reported instruction-following percent.

items.label: MMMU Pro Standard 10
items.detail: Vendor-reported multimodal benchmark percent.

items.label: VoiceBench
items.detail: Vendor-reported audio benchmark percent with a formatting instruction.

items.label: StrongREJECT
items.detail: Vendor-reported safety benchmark percent.

visuals.interpretation: The main table is broad coverage evidence, not a single composite score. Each number belongs to a different benchmark definition and evaluation setup.
limitations: The values are vendor-published and should not be read as a common scale.
The table does not identify evaluated checkpoint precision, and some provider surfaces disagree on other values.
visuals.alt_text: Result constellation of selected vendor-reported effort 0.99 scores: 29.7 on Humanity's Last Exam without tools, 77.6 SWE-bench Verified, 79.8 IFBench, 73.5 MMMU Pro Standard 10, 91.4 VoiceBench, and 98.6 StrongREJECT. These benchmarks are not one common scale.
visuals.fallback: At effort 0.99, the provider reports 29.7 on Humanity's Last Exam without tools, 77.6 on SWE-bench Verified, 79.8 on IFBench, 73.5 on MMMU Pro Standard 10, 91.4 on VoiceBench, and 98.6 on StrongREJECT. The benchmark families are not directly comparable.

visuals.title: Supported context is not demonstrated accuracy
visuals.question: Which long-context statements are established, and which remain open?

items.label: Provider capacity claim
items.detail: The content-addressed provider pages advertise support for a context window up to one million tokens.

items.label: Hosted access options
items.detail: The cited Tinker access surface exposes 64K and 256K context options rather than a one-million-token option.

items.label: Accuracy boundary
items.detail: The cited sources do not establish useful accuracy across every position of a one-million-token prompt.

items.label: Checkpoint boundary
items.detail: The sources also do not establish BF16 and NVFP4 quality parity or unchanged safety after downstream fine-tuning.

visuals.interpretation: A declared maximum capacity, a hosted service limit, and measured long-context accuracy are three different claims. Only the first two are directly described by the cited provider surfaces.
limitations: This boundary view does not add a new long-context evaluation.
Hosted options and model capability may change after the content-addressed retrieval date.
visuals.alt_text: Four lanes distinguish the provider's one-million-token capacity statement, hosted 64K and 256K options, the absence of demonstrated accuracy throughout one million tokens, and the absence of BF16-versus-NVFP4 quality parity evidence.
visuals.fallback: The provider advertises up to one million tokens, while the cited hosted options expose 64K and 256K. Neither statement demonstrates useful accuracy across the full million-token window, and the sources do not establish BF16 and NVFP4 quality parity.

visuals.title: Reproducibility depends on fixed source identities
visuals.question: Which Inkling sources can be revisited exactly?

items.label: Provider website snapshot
items.detail: The exact model-card, release-page, and Acceptable Use Policy HTML retrieved on 2026-07-18 is fixed by SHA-256 and byte count; the live pages can still change.

items.label: BF16 checkpoint card
items.detail: The initial release is pinned to its immutable repository commit rather than a moving branch.

items.label: NVFP4 checkpoint card
items.detail: The quantized release is pinned to a separate initial commit with its own hardware and numeric-mode statements.

items.label: Unresolved disagreement
items.detail: The fixed website and initial checkpoint cards disagree on values including GDPVal-AA and MMMU Pro; source identity preserves the conflict but does not resolve it.

visuals.interpretation: Content hashes and commit pins make each cited surface reproducible. They do not make contradictory vendor values agree; they make the disagreement auditable.
limitations: The matrix does not infer which disagreeing value the provider intended as canonical.
Later live-page revisions require new hashes and a new editorial review.
visuals.alt_text: Evidence matrix distinguishes content-addressed provider HTML, pinned BF16 and NVFP4 checkpoint cards, and an unresolved disagreement between those fixed sources. Hashes preserve the exact evidence but do not decide which conflicting value is canonical.
visuals.fallback: The website HTML is fixed by retrieval hash and byte count, while BF16 and NVFP4 checkpoint cards are fixed by separate initial commits. Those source identities make disagreements reproducible but do not resolve which value is canonical.

visuals.title: One token activates only part of Inkling
visuals.question: How can Inkling store 975 billion parameters while using 41 billion on one token path?

items.label: Stored model
items.detail: Inkling has 975 billion total parameters across its sparse mixture-of-experts architecture.

items.label: Routed branch
items.detail: For each token, the sparse feed-forward path selects 6 of 256 routed experts rather than activating every routed expert.

items.label: Shared branch
items.detail: The same token also uses 2 shared experts that are always active.

items.label: Active token path
items.detail: The selected routed experts and the 2 shared experts make up a 41-billion-parameter active path for that token.

visuals.interpretation: Total parameters describe everything stored in the sparse model, while active parameters describe the much smaller expert subset used along one token's route. The two numbers answer different capacity questions and should not be treated as interchangeable.
limitations: This partition is conceptual and does not draw the physical layout, size, or specialization of individual experts.
The 41-billion figure describes active parameters on a token path, not the memory needed to store the full checkpoint.
The visual does not show attention layers, modality encoders, training, or expert-routing quality.
visuals.alt_text: Partition of one Inkling token path. The full sparse model stores 975 billion parameters. A token uses 6 selected experts from a pool of 256 routed experts and also uses 2 shared experts, producing a 41-billion-parameter active path.
visuals.fallback: Inkling stores 975 billion total parameters. For one token, its sparse feed-forward path selects 6 of 256 routed experts and also uses 2 shared experts. Those active components account for the reported 41-billion-parameter token path. Total and active parameters therefore describe different parts of the architecture.

visuals.title: BF16 and NVFP4 require different deployment plans
visuals.question: What changes when a local deployment chooses the BF16 or NVFP4 checkpoint?

items.label: BF16 checkpoint
items.detail: The content-addressed model-card snapshot lists at least 2 TB of aggregate VRAM and gives 8 NVIDIA B300 or 16 H200 GPUs as examples. The initial release is pinned to commit 91b051f1ec836e6d56596c624c3775b495d797b1.

items.label: NVFP4 with W4A4
items.detail: The snapshot lists a 600 GB aggregate minimum and 4 B300 GPUs, with SM100-or-newer architecture required. The NVFP4 release is pinned to commit 93a182fb0376affeaeecfa4658c37a0fe9e5fa9e.

items.label: NVFP4 with W4A16
items.detail: The same 600 GB aggregate minimum is paired with 8 H200 GPUs for this numeric mode, using the same pinned NVFP4 checkpoint.

items.label: Quality comparison
items.detail: The release does not establish that NVFP4 preserves BF16 benchmark quality, and the main benchmark table does not identify its evaluated weight precision.

visuals.interpretation: Quantization lowers the reported aggregate-memory requirement, but it also introduces hardware- and numeric-mode-specific constraints. Memory fit alone cannot establish that the quantized checkpoint reproduces the published quality claims.
limitations: The GPU configurations are provider-listed examples from the content-addressed 2026-07-18 model-card retrieval, not independently benchmarked recommendations.
The live model card remains mutable; the checkpoint identities are pinned to their initial release commits.
No BF16-versus-NVFP4 quality, throughput, latency, or cost comparison is available in the cited sources.
visuals.alt_text: Checkpoint comparison for local Inkling deployment. BF16 is listed at a 2-terabyte aggregate VRAM minimum with 8 B300 or 16 H200 GPUs. NVFP4 is listed at 600 gigabytes: W4A4 on 4 B300 GPUs with SM100-or-newer architecture, or W4A16 on 8 H200 GPUs. Quality parity is not established.
visuals.fallback: The model-card snapshot lists BF16 at a minimum of 2 TB aggregate VRAM, with 8 B300 or 16 H200 GPUs, and pins the release to commit 91b051f1ec836e6d56596c624c3775b495d797b1. It lists NVFP4 at 600 GB: W4A4 on 4 B300 GPUs with SM100-or-newer architecture, or W4A16 on 8 H200 GPUs, pinned to commit 93a182fb0376affeaeecfa4658c37a0fe9e5fa9e. The sources do not establish benchmark parity.

visuals.title: Inkling's claims come from non-equivalent evidence surfaces
visuals.question: Which source and checkpoint does each major Inkling claim actually describe?

items.label: Provider pages and use terms
items.detail: Content-addressed HTML retrieved on 2026-07-18 dates the release to July 15, labels the model Apache 2.0, and links an Acceptable Use Policy that also conditions use. The live pages remain mutable.

items.label: Core architecture
items.detail: The model card and release describe 66 layers, 975 billion total parameters, 41 billion active parameters, 6 of 256 routed experts per token, and 2 shared experts.

items.label: Context claim and hosted access
items.detail: The provider advertises up to 1 million tokens, while Tinker offers 64K and 256K options. Neither statement establishes useful accuracy throughout a 1-million-token prompt.

items.label: Released checkpoints
items.detail: BF16 and NVFP4 are separate initial-release commits with different storage formats and hardware requirements. The sources do not establish quality parity between them.

items.label: Main benchmark table
items.detail: The reported scores use effort 0.99, but the table does not identify the evaluated weight precision and combines benchmark families that are not one common measurement scale.

items.label: Other evaluation checkpoints
items.detail: The effort chart's Humanity's Last Exam points came from an earlier checkpoint, and forecasting used a checkpoint different from the released one.

items.label: Unresolved source disagreement
items.detail: The content-addressed website snapshot and immutable initial checkpoint cards disagree on some values, including GDPVal-AA and MMMU Pro, without identifying one canonical set.

visuals.interpretation: Architecture, hosted context, downloadable weight formats, benchmark tables, and other evaluation checkpoints describe different objects. The evidence remains reproducible only when each claim stays attached to its content-addressed page or pinned checkpoint identity.
limitations: The matrix does not rank the sources or resolve their benchmark-value disagreement.
It does not imply that BF16 and NVFP4 have equal quality or that advertised context capacity demonstrates full-window accuracy.
The benchmark rows preserve checkpoint and precision uncertainty instead of treating all vendor-reported scores as properties of one released artifact.
Provider-page claims refer only to the SHA-256-identified HTML retrieved on 2026-07-18; later live-page changes require new review.
visuals.alt_text: Evidence matrix separating Inkling's mutable provider pages, core architecture, advertised and hosted context limits, pinned BF16 and NVFP4 checkpoints, main effort-0.99 benchmark table, earlier or different evaluation checkpoints, and unresolved website-versus-checkpoint-card value discrepancies.
visuals.fallback: Inkling's evidence surfaces are not interchangeable. Content-addressed provider pages support the release, architecture, context, license label, and linked use-policy statements. BF16 and NVFP4 are separate pinned commits with unestablished quality parity. The main benchmark table uses effort 0.99 but does not identify precision. Some effort and forecasting results use earlier or different checkpoints, and the website snapshot disagrees with initial checkpoint cards on some published values.

glossary.definition: A model design that routes each token through a selected subset of feed-forward expert networks rather than activating every expert.
The parameters used along a token's routed computation path. This differs from the total parameters stored in all experts.
A specific saved set of model weights. Results from one checkpoint do not automatically describe another checkpoint or a later fine-tuned derivative.
The low-precision checkpoint format offered for Inkling to reduce aggregate VRAM requirements relative to BF16.
A control that changes how many generated reasoning tokens the model tends to use, allowing a trade between token use and measured performance.
The prompts, tools, execution environment, grading rules, and limits used to run an evaluation. Different harnesses can make nominally similar scores difficult to compare directly.

revision_notes: Initial review-required explainer fixture for the July 15, 2026 Inkling release.

paper_version v1 is a local Paper Atlas record for the initial release; the provider's mutable model card, release page, and Acceptable Use Policy are tied to exact official HTML retrieved on 2026-07-18.

Model card retrieval: SHA-256 fe653ffb5f4b9f54f011491f60cd8d6b9885d667484880d4566d76827f22a7e9, 65,631 bytes. Release page: SHA-256 cb28c6a6c8c47c68f55f2c636481bf35a1b9f5a349e5f00148c583fafbc138fc, 222,133 bytes.
Acceptable Use Policy retrieval: SHA-256 c62535263733dbeabb838ff881850928a878bc5c539ce1401a59a237bbf5c2e7, 25,968 bytes. Later live-page changes require a new content hash and review.

The BF16 repository is pinned to initial release commit 91b051f1ec836e6d56596c624c3775b495d797b1 and the NVFP4 repository to initial release commit 93a182fb0376affeaeecfa4658c37a0fe9e5fa9e; the mutable website card and immutable checkpoint cards contain small unresolved benchmark-value differences.
Apache 2.0 is recorded together with the provider's linked Acceptable Use Policy rather than presented as unrestricted use.
The visual is an original evidence matrix; no provider figure is reproduced.
